does: This paper proposes SPO-VLM, a two-stage defense framework against adversarial jailbreaks in vision language models that combines activation steering with preference optimization. Stage I computes adaptive, layer-specific steering vectors from diverse data sources to suppress harmful behaviors during inference without relying on task-specific contrastive prompts. Stage II refines these vectors through sequence-level preference optimization using automated toxicity assessment and visual-consistency rewards. The approach addresses limitations of existing activation steering methods that often degrade visual grounding while remaining vulnerable to attacks. The framework targets the inherent robustness vulnerabilities of VLMs that combine visual and textual understanding, aiming to maintain both safety and model performance across diverse inputs.
connects: This work builds on the emerging activation steering literature, which has shown promise as a defense mechanism by intervening at the activation level rather than through retraining. It responds to limitations in prior approaches that extract harmful directions using contrastive prompts, which lack generalization across attacks. The paper also connects to broader work on alignment and preference optimization in language models, adapting sequence-level preference optimization techniques from the LLM domain to the multimodal setting. It engages with adversarial robustness research in vision-language models and draws on toxicity detection methods for automated evaluation. The dual-stage approach combines insights from both mechanistic interpretability work on steering and reinforcement learning from human feedback paradigms.
enables: This defense mechanism enables more robust deployment of VLMs in safety-critical applications where adversarial inputs or jailbreak attempts are plausible threats. The adaptive, data-driven approach to computing steering vectors without task-specific prompts could facilitate scaling defense mechanisms across different model architectures and domains. The integration of visual-consistency rewards points toward future work on ensuring that safety interventions do not compromise core model capabilities, which is essential for practical adoption. The framework could inspire follow-up research combining activation-level and optimization-level defenses in other multimodal systems, as well as investigations into the theoretical properties of steering vectors across vision-language architectures and their transferability across model variants.
